findings.title: No Express Checkout Buttons in Cart Drawer or Buy-Box

evidence_anchors.context: Header shows cart icon (top-right) with no visible Apple Pay, Google Pay, or Shop Pay buttons anywhere above the fold. No express checkout entry point is accessible on initial page load.

reference_citations.section: Finding 6: Place Wallet Buttons Early — Top of Checkout, Not Bottom
Finding 1: Digital Wallets Lift Checkout Conversion 7-22%

findings.observation: The cart drawer element (e17) is present in the page DOM but was empty at capture — no Apple Pay, Google Pay, or Shop Pay buttons appear inside it. No express checkout buttons exist anywhere in the above-fold header area or on the product cards in the featured collection. On mobile, where 70%+ of Shop Pay transactions originate, the absence of wallet buttons means every buyer must navigate through the full checkout form regardless of whether their payment credentials are already stored on their device.
findings.recommendation: On a Shopify store, enable the 'dynamic checkout buttons' setting on product forms and within the cart drawer. This surfaces Apple Pay, Google Pay, or Shop Pay (whichever is relevant to the shopper's device and configured wallets) as a single-tap payment path above the standard 'Checkout' button. Place these buttons at the top of the cart drawer, before the email or address fields, so mobile visitors can complete purchase in 1-2 taps from any page.
findings.why_this_matters: Stripe's controlled A/B testing shows express checkout buttons placed at the top of the flow convert at approximately 2x the rate of buttons placed at the end. Apple Pay specifically delivers a +22.3% conversion lift (Stripe verified). For a mobile-first automotive accessories store where the average visitor is browsing on an iPhone or Android device with a digital wallet already configured, the missing express path is the single highest-leverage checkout fix available.

findings.title: Free Shipping Threshold Visible Above the Fold

evidence_anchors.context: Blue announcement bar at top of page reads 'FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+' with a truck emoji. Fully visible above the fold without scrolling at mobile viewport.

reference_citations.section: Finding 1: Extra Costs Are the #1 Abandonment Driver

findings.observation: The page leads with a blue announcement bar reading 'FREE SHIPPING on most orders $75+' visible at scroll_y=0 without any user interaction. Visitors understand the shipping cost structure before they add any product to cart, removing the primary trigger for late-funnel abandonment.
findings.recommendation: The current implementation is effective. If the store offers free shipping on all orders (not just 'most'), updating the copy to remove the qualifying word 'most' would eliminate residual uncertainty. If 'most' reflects a genuine product exclusion (e.g., oversized exhaust systems), consider adding a tooltip or link clarifying which items are excluded so there are no surprises at checkout.
findings.why_this_matters: Baymard's multi-year research finds 39% of actionable cart abandonment is triggered by extra costs appearing late. Surfacing the shipping threshold on the first visible row of the page is the single most direct treatment for this abandonment cause.

findings.title: Payment Method Icons Footer-Only — Not Surfaced Pre-Cart

element.text_content: American Express Apple Pay Discover Google Pay Mastercard

evidence_anchors.context: Footer shows 8 payment method logos: American Express, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard, PayPal, Shop Pay, Visa. These are display-only icons in the footer at scroll_y ~1629, well below the fold and outside the purchase path.

reference_citations.section: Finding 14: Payment Method Visibility Before Checkout
Finding 13: Limited Payment Methods Cause 10% Abandonment

findings.observation: The store lists 8 payment methods (American Express, Apple Pay, Discover, Google Pay, Mastercard, PayPal, Shop Pay, Visa) as icon badges in the footer at approximately scroll_y 1629 — visible only after the visitor has scrolled past the newsletter section. Payment variety is strong, meeting the 4-method minimum with significant margin, but the icons appear only after the fold. No payment method indicators appear near the product cards, in the cart drawer, or anywhere in the purchase path. A visitor who is unsure whether their preferred payment method is accepted must scroll to the very bottom of the page to verify.
findings.recommendation: Add payment method icons or a brief 'Accepts:' line near product-card buy buttons or within the cart drawer summary. On Shopify, this can be done by adding the payment-methods Liquid snippet to the cart drawer template. Showing familiar logos (especially PayPal and Shop Pay) close to the 'Add to Cart' action reassures hesitant buyers before they commit to clicking through to checkout. The icons do not need to be interactive — static display near the CTA is sufficient.
findings.why_this_matters: Stripe's testing found that surfacing at least one additional relevant payment method beyond cards drove a 7.4% conversion increase and 12% revenue increase on average. When a shopper cannot quickly confirm that PayPal or Shop Pay is available, they face a micro-uncertainty that adds friction to the decision to proceed.

findings.title: Cart Drawer Lacks Shipping Progress and Subtotal Confirmation

evidence_anchors.context: Cart icon visible top-right of header (e6); cart drawer element (e17) is present in DOM as a sticky custom element but was empty at page-load capture, indicating drawer opens on interaction. Drawer contents — including any subtotal, item count, shipping progress bar, or express checkout — were not visible at capture state.

reference_citations.section: Finding 18: Cart Summary Visibility During Checkout
Finding 4: Shipping Cost Timing — Early vs. Late Disclosure

findings.observation: The cart drawer (e17) exists as a custom element in the page DOM but was empty at capture. The DOM slice does not reveal whether the open drawer displays a subtotal, a 'free shipping progress' bar toward the $75 threshold, or a running estimated total. Without a shipping progress bar inside the drawer (e.g., 'Add $X more for free shipping'), the announcement bar's threshold message is disconnected from the in-cart experience — the shopper sees the threshold once on load but gets no feedback on how close they are as items are added.
findings.recommendation: Add a free-shipping progress bar to the cart drawer that shows the gap between the current cart subtotal and the $75 free shipping threshold (e.g., 'You're $23 away from free shipping'). This is a native feature in several Shopify cart drawer apps and available as a Liquid/JavaScript addition to the Dawn and related themes. The bar should update dynamically as items are added or removed. Additionally, confirm that the open drawer shows a clear subtotal before the 'Checkout' button so shoppers see the total before committing to the checkout flow.
findings.why_this_matters: Baymard's research identifies 39-48% of actionable cart abandonment as cost-surprise driven. A real-time progress bar toward the free shipping threshold directly motivates shoppers to increase cart size while simultaneously signaling that the final checkout cost will not surprise them, reducing the main abandonment trigger at the cart stage.

notes: Page type is homepage — checkout form fields, inline validation, progress indicators, and trust signals near payment field are outside scope for this page type. Audit scoped to: express checkout button presence, cart drawer signals, and shipping threshold framing per cluster guidance.

No cookie consent banner was detected (capture_state.overlays_detected=[]). This is consistent with a US-audience store (awdmods.com, .com domain, USD pricing, no hreflang). Cookie consent findings are not applicable without EU/UK market signals.

Cart drawer was empty at acquisition capture — the drawer contents on interaction could not be verified from the static capture. The PARTIAL finding (F-4) reflects uncertainty about the open-drawer state, not a confirmed absence. A live test opening the cart would resolve this finding.
Payment method variety is strong (8 methods including Apple Pay, Google Pay, Shop Pay, PayPal, all major cards). The gap is positioning — logos are footer-only rather than proximate to CTAs.

The 'Made to Order' badge visible on the VelourTex floor mats product card (e28, section-3-mobile.jpg) implies custom lead times. This is relevant context for the checkout-flows cluster: if delivery dates are not displayed at checkout for made-to-order items, that represents an abandonment risk (Baymard: 21% abandon over slow delivery). This cannot be verified from the homepage capture alone.
No exit-intent overlay, cart-expiry messaging, or push/browser notification prompt was detected in the capture state.
